title: Introduction
content_preview: The answer outlines the political and constitutional crises in Pakistan from 1947-1956 and post-2008, reflecting structural weaknesses in governance.

title: 1947-1956: A State Without a Stable Political Compass
content_preview: This period saw Pakistan facing severe instability due to leadership vacuum after Quaid-e-Azam's death in 1948 and Liaquat Ali Khan's assassination, leading to four prime ministers in four years; cons

title: Leadership Vacuum post Independence
content_preview: Post-independence, the early deaths of key leaders left Pakistan rudderless, with frequent changes in prime ministers.

title: Constitutional Delay and Institutional Paralysis
content_preview: Pakistan lacked a permanent constitution for nine years, operating under the interim 1935 Act, causing institutional stagnation.

title: Arbitrary Executive Authority Undermined Parliament
content_preview: Executives like Governor-General Ghulam Muhammad dismissed elected leaders despite parliamentary support, weakening democratic institutions.

title: Ethnic Disunity due to Forced Provincial Mergers
content_preview: The 1955 One Unit scheme merged West Pakistan provinces, sparking resentment in Balochistan and Sindh.

title: Judiciary Enabled Undemocratic Rule
content_preview: In the 1955 Tamizuddin Case, the court validated the dissolution of the Constituent Assembly by an unelected Governor-General.

title: Post 2008: A Democracy Captured by Power Struggles and Legal Battles
content_preview: Post-2008, Pakistan's democracy faced civil-military tensions, judicial overreach, weak coalitions, politicized accountability, and constitutional conflicts between judiciary and executive.

title: Civil-Military Conflict behind Democratic Facade
content_preview: The 2011 Memogate Scandal revealed civilian fears of military coups, as seen after Osama bin Laden's killing.

title: Judicial Overreach into Political Domain
content_preview: In 2017, PM Nawaz Sharif was disqualified over a minor iqama issue from Panama leaks, viewed as politically motivated.

title: Weak and Divided Coalition Governments
content_preview: The 2022 PDM alliance struggled with internal contradictions, hindering consistent governance and reforms.

title: Politicized Accountability Undermines Rule of Law
content_preview: The NAB disproportionately targeted opposition leaders during PTI rule, criticized by HRCP in 2020.

title: Constitutional Conflict between Judiciary and Executive
content_preview: The 2023 election deadlock in Punjab and KP highlighted failures to enforce constitutional timelines under Article 224 due to political interference.

title: Broader Structural Weaknesses in Governance Across Both Eras
content_preview: Both periods exhibit institutional discontinuity with 14 prime ministers since 1947; absence of checks allowing excessive ordinances; overdependence on unelected institutions like the military; and we

title: Lack of Institutional Continuity
content_preview: Frequent parliamentary ousters and regime changes undermine political sustainability.

title: Absence of Checks and Balances
content_preview: Leaders from Ghulam Muhammad to Imran Khan relied excessively on ordinances.

title: Overdependence on Unelected Institutions
content_preview: The military's influence, via ISPR, shapes political narratives over civilian decisions.

title: Weak Local Government System
content_preview: Grassroots democracy is ignored, with no local elections held in Punjab from 2016 to 2022.

title: Critical Appraisal
content_preview: A critical evaluation of the structural issues in Pakistani governance across the eras.

title: Conclusion
content_preview: The crises reflect enduring structural weaknesses in Pakistan's governance, calling for institutional reforms.
